---
title: "SVD in Action: Low-Rank Approximation and the Pseudoinverse"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# One restrained visual style for every figure in this article, matched to the
# rest of the Geometry-of-Data track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

VEC    = "#2b3a67"   # generic vectors / data
HILITE = "#c1121f"   # leading singular direction / truncation
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"   # second direction / highlights / minimum-norm solution
GREY   = "#6c757d"
PALE   = "#9fb3d1"


def draw_vec(ax, tail, head, color=VEC, lw=2.4, label=None, z=5,
             label_offset=(0.0, 0.0), ls="-"):
    """Draw a 2-D arrow from tail to head with a clean head."""
    ax.annotate("", xy=head, xytext=tail,
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=color, lw=lw,
                                shrinkA=0, shrinkB=0, linestyle=ls), zorder=z)
    if label is not None:
        ax.text(head[0] + label_offset[0], head[1] + label_offset[1], label,
                color=color, fontsize=13, ha="center", va="center", zorder=z + 1)
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the second summit of Movement V and the place
> the **singular value decomposition** finally earns its keep. It assumes the SVD itself
> from [GEOM-20]: every real $m \times n$ matrix factors as $A = U\Sigma V^\top$, with
> $U$ ($m\times m$) and $V$ ($n\times n$) **orthogonal** ($U^\top U = I$, $V^\top V = I$)
> and $\Sigma$ ($m\times n$) holding the **singular values**
> $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \dots \ge \sigma_r > 0$ on its diagonal, where $r$ is the
> rank; equivalently $A = \sum_{i=1}^r \sigma_i u_i v_i^\top$, a sum of rank-one pieces.
> It leans on **orthogonal projection** and the closest-point property ([GEOM-06]), on
> **solving $Ax=b$** and what "no solution" / "infinitely many solutions" mean ([GEOM-11]),
> on **rank and the four subspaces** ([GEOM-12]), on the **transpose** algebra
> ([GEOM-10]), and on the **dot product / norm** $\|x\|^2 = x^\top x$ ([GEOM-04]). The one
> question: *once we have the SVD, how do we get the best low-rank approximation of a
> matrix, and a "solve" for systems that have no inverse?* It hands the **PCA machinery**
> to Unsupervised Learning (which owns the *statistics*; we own the algebra) and the
> **pseudoinverse** to Regression for rank-deficient designs ([REGR-05], [REGR-14]).

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

The SVD writes any matrix as an ordered sum of rank-one pieces,
$A = \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sigma_i u_i v_i^\top$, each weighted by a singular value
$\sigma_i$. This article defends a single claim with two faces. **Keep the top $k$
pieces and you get the best rank-$k$ approximation of $A$ there is** — provably best, in
both the Frobenius and spectral norms — with the error equal to the singular values you
threw away. That is the **Eckart–Young** theorem, and it is why "truncate the SVD" is
the right move for compression, denoising, and latent-structure discovery. **Invert the
pieces you keep and you get the Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse** $A^+ = V\Sigma^+U^\top$,
the one object that "solves" $Ax=b$ for *every* $A$ — square or rectangular, full-rank or
deficient — returning the least-squares fit when there is no exact solution and the
minimum-norm choice when there are infinitely many. Both faces are the same act of
reading a matrix one singular value at a time.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Order a matrix's action by importance — the SVD does exactly this, ranking it as a sum of
rank-one layers $\sigma_1 u_1 v_1^\top \succ \sigma_2 u_2 v_2^\top \succ \dots$, biggest
first. **Truncate** the sum after $k$ layers and you have the best rank-$k$ approximation
of the matrix that exists; the error you incur is precisely the singular values you
dropped. **Invert** each kept layer (replace $\sigma_i$ by $1/\sigma_i$) and you have the
**pseudoinverse**, which returns the smallest least-squares solution to $Ax=b$ no matter
how degenerate $A$ is. One decomposition, two indispensable tools, both just "use the big
singular values, discard the small ones."
:::

Everything in this article is the working-out of that box. The two halves —
approximation and inversion — look unrelated at first, but they are mirror images. To
*approximate* you keep the large $\sigma_i$ and zero the small ones; to *invert* you keep
the large $\sigma_i$ (as $1/\sigma_i$) and zero the small ones. The same dividing line
between "big enough to trust" and "too small to matter" runs through both, and that line
is where this article spends its judgment.

# Why we are here: two problems the inverse could not solve {#sec-why}

The SVD was completed as a piece of pure linear algebra by Eugenio Beltrami (1873) and
Camille Jordan (1874), then rediscovered and extended over the following decades. But the
two uses we build here were forced into existence by concrete failures of the ordinary
inverse — and keeping those failures in view is what makes every step below feel
inevitable rather than clever.

## The first failure: there is too much matrix {#sec-why-toobig}

A matrix is often a *table of measurements*: rows are objects (images, documents, users),
columns are features (pixels, words, items). Such tables are huge and, crucially,
**redundant** — columns are correlated, rows repeat patterns, the "real" information lives
in far fewer dimensions than the raw shape suggests. We would like to *compress* the table
to its essential content: replace the full $m \times n$ matrix with a much smaller object
that captures almost all of it. The honest question is **which** small object — of all the
rank-$k$ matrices (matrices whose action only spans $k$ directions), which one is closest
to $A$? The inverse says nothing about this; it is the wrong tool. The SVD answers it
exactly, and that answer is Eckart–Young (@sec-eckart-young).

## The second failure: there is no inverse {#sec-why-noinverse}

Photogrammetry, geodesy, statistics — the same over-determined predicament that birthed
least squares (the Regression track's [REGR-00]) — repeatedly hands us a system $Ax = b$
where $A$ is **not square**, or is square but **singular**. There is no $A^{-1}$. Three
distinct things can go wrong, and a usable tool must handle all three at once:

- **Tall and thin** ($m > n$, more equations than unknowns): usually *no* exact solution,
  because $b$ does not lie in the reach of $A$. We want the $x$ that comes *closest* —
  least squares.
- **Short and wide** ($m < n$, more unknowns than equations): usually *infinitely many*
  exact solutions, a whole subspace of them. We want a principled way to pick *one*.
- **Rank-deficient** (columns redundant, the multicollinearity of [REGR-07]): the matrix
  $A^\top A$ that the normal equations of [GEOM-06] rely on is itself singular, so the
  classic formula $\hat x = (A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top b$ blows up.

E. H. Moore (1920) and, independently, Roger Penrose (1955) defined a single object — the
**pseudoinverse** $A^+$ — that does the right thing in *every* one of these cases:
returns the least-squares solution when there is no exact one, the minimum-norm solution
when there are many, and the ordinary $A^{-1}$ when $A$ is invertible. Penrose's
construction is exactly "invert the SVD layer by layer," which is why this object belongs
here, right after the SVD. We build it from scratch in @sec-pinv.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why not just always use the normal-equations formula $(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top b$ from the
projection article?"* Because $A^\top A$ is invertible **only when $A$ has full column
rank** — no redundant columns. The moment two predictors are collinear (or you have more
features than data points), $A^\top A$ is singular and that formula is undefined. The
pseudoinverse is the repair: it agrees with $(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top$ exactly when that
exists, and keeps working — by picking the minimum-norm solution — when it does not. So
the pseudoinverse is not an alternative to least-squares projection; it is its completion
to the degenerate cases [GEOM-06] could not reach.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Recall the one-line geometry of the SVD from [GEOM-20]: a matrix $A$ acts on the unit
sphere by **rotating** (via $V^\top$), **stretching** along orthogonal axes by the
factors $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \dots$ (via $\Sigma$), then **rotating again** (via
$U$). The image of the sphere is an ellipsoid whose axis *lengths* are exactly the
singular values. The longest axis is $\sigma_1$, the next $\sigma_2$, and so on.

That picture makes both halves of this article visual before they are algebraic.

- **Low-rank approximation** is *flattening the thin axes of the ellipsoid*. A direction
  with a tiny singular value barely contributes to where $A$ sends things; collapse it to
  zero and the ellipsoid hardly changes. Keep only the $k$ longest axes and you have the
  best $k$-dimensional shadow of the full transformation. The error is governed by the
  axes you flattened — the small $\sigma$'s.
- **The pseudoinverse** is *running the ellipsoid backward*. To undo $A$ you un-rotate,
  un-stretch (divide by each $\sigma_i$), and un-rotate. Dividing by a long axis is fine.
  Dividing by a near-zero axis is catastrophic — it amplifies noise without bound — so the
  honest "inverse" *refuses* to invert the collapsed directions and leaves them alone. The
  pseudoinverse is the inverse that knows which directions it is allowed to touch.

@fig-ellipse makes this concrete: one matrix, its singular-axis ellipse, the rank-1
flattening, and the directions the pseudoinverse can and cannot undo.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ellipse
#| fig-cap: 'One $2\times 2$ matrix read through its SVD. **Left:** $A$ sends the unit circle (grey) to an ellipse (blue) whose semi-axes are the singular vectors $u_1,u_2$ scaled by $\sigma_1 > \sigma_2$. **Center:** the best rank-1 approximation $A_1=\sigma_1 u_1 v_1^\top$ collapses the short axis entirely — the circle maps onto a *line segment* along $u_1$. The dropped axis had length $\sigma_2$, exactly the approximation error. **Right:** to invert, the pseudoinverse divides by each axis length; the long axis ($\div\sigma_1$) is safe, the short axis ($\div\sigma_2$) is the fragile one that amplifies error — and would be *refused* outright if $\sigma_2$ were zero.'

A = np.array([[2.0, 1.1],
              [0.4, 1.7]])
U, s, Vt = np.linalg.svd(A)
theta = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 240)
circle = np.vstack([np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)])
ellipse = A @ circle
A1 = s[0] * np.outer(U[:, 0], Vt[0, :])   # best rank-1
seg = A1 @ circle

fig, (a0, a1, a2) = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(9.2, 3.3))

a0.plot(circle[0], circle[1], color=GREY, lw=1.4, label="unit circle")
a0.plot(ellipse[0], ellipse[1], color=VEC, lw=2.2, label="$A(\\mathrm{circle})$")
draw_vec(a0, (0, 0), tuple(s[0]*U[:, 0]), color=HILITE, label="$\\sigma_1 u_1$", label_offset=(0.3, 0.25))
draw_vec(a0, (0, 0), tuple(s[1]*U[:, 1]), color=ACCENT, label="$\\sigma_2 u_2$", label_offset=(-0.45, 0.1))
a0.set_title("$A$: circle $\\to$ ellipse"); a0.set_aspect("equal"); a0.legend(fontsize=8, loc="lower right")

a1.plot(ellipse[0], ellipse[1], color=PALE, lw=1.2, ls="--", label="full $A$")
a1.plot(seg[0], seg[1], color=HILITE, lw=3.0, label="rank-1 $A_1$")
a1.set_title("Best rank-1: short axis flattened"); a1.set_aspect("equal"); a1.legend(fontsize=8, loc="lower right")

a2.plot(circle[0], circle[1], color=GREY, lw=1.4)
draw_vec(a2, (0, 0), tuple(U[:, 0]/s[0]), color=HILITE, label="$u_1/\\sigma_1$ (safe)", label_offset=(0.15, 0.28))
draw_vec(a2, (0, 0), tuple(U[:, 1]/s[1]), color=ACCENT, label="$u_2/\\sigma_2$ (fragile)", label_offset=(-0.1, 0.32))
a2.set_title("Inverting: $\\div\\sigma_i$ per axis"); a2.set_aspect("equal"); a2.legend(fontsize=8, loc="lower left")

for ax in (a0, a1, a2):
    ax.set_xlim(-3, 3); ax.set_ylim(-3, 3)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"singular values: sigma_1 = {s[0]:.4f},  sigma_2 = {s[1]:.4f}")
```

# Part I — Low-Rank Approximation {#sec-lowrank}

## The rank-one layers, and what "rank $k$" buys {#sec-layers}

Start from the outer-product form of the SVD established in [GEOM-20]. With orthonormal
left singular vectors $u_1,\dots,u_r$ (columns of $U$), orthonormal right singular vectors
$v_1,\dots,v_r$ (columns of $V$), and singular values
$\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \dots \ge \sigma_r > 0$,

$$
A \;=\; U\Sigma V^\top \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sigma_i\, u_i v_i^\top .
$$ {#eq-svd-outer}

Each term $u_i v_i^\top$ is an **outer product**: $u_i$ is $m\times 1$, $v_i^\top$ is
$1\times n$, so $u_i v_i^\top$ is a full $m \times n$ matrix — but a very thin one. Every
column of $u_i v_i^\top$ is a scalar multiple of the single vector $u_i$ (the column $j$
is $(v_i)_j\, u_i$), so its column space is the *one* line spanned by $u_i$: it has
**rank one**. Equation @eq-svd-outer therefore writes $A$ as a weighted sum of $r$
rank-one matrices, ordered so the heaviest weight $\sigma_1$ comes first.

The **rank-$k$ truncation** keeps the first $k$ of these layers:

$$
A_k \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sigma_i\, u_i v_i^\top
\;=\; U\,\Sigma_k\,V^\top,
\qquad k \le r,
$$ {#eq-Ak}

where $\Sigma_k$ is $\Sigma$ with all but its top $k$ singular values set to zero. Because
$A_k$ is a sum of $k$ rank-one pieces built on the *orthonormal* vectors $u_1,\dots,u_k$,
its column space is exactly $\operatorname{span}\{u_1,\dots,u_k\}$ — a $k$-dimensional
space — so $A_k$ has rank exactly $k$ (assuming $\sigma_k > 0$). It is a genuine rank-$k$
matrix, and the claim of Part I is that **no rank-$k$ matrix is closer to $A$ than this
one.**

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"How much storage does $A_k$ save?"* The full matrix $A$ costs $mn$ numbers. The
truncation $A_k$ is determined entirely by the kept pieces: $k$ left vectors ($mk$
numbers), $k$ right vectors ($nk$ numbers), and $k$ singular values, for a total of
$k(m + n + 1)$. When $k \ll \min(m,n)$ this is dramatically smaller — a
$1000\times1000$ image ($10^6$ numbers) approximated at rank $50$ needs about
$50\cdot 2001 \approx 10^5$, a tenfold saving — *and* it is the **best** such
compression at that budget, not merely *a* compression. That "best" is the whole point;
without it, low-rank truncation would just be one lossy scheme among many.
:::

## Measuring the error: the Frobenius norm {#sec-frob}

To say "$A_k$ is closest to $A$" we need a way to measure the distance between two
matrices. The natural one treats a matrix as a long list of $mn$ numbers and uses ordinary
Euclidean length. This is the **Frobenius norm**.

::: {#def-frob}
**Definition (Frobenius norm).** For an $m\times n$ matrix $M$ with entries $M_{ij}$,
$$
\|M\|_F \;=\; \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}^2}\, .
$$
The squared norm $\|M\|_F^2 = \sum_{i,j}M_{ij}^2$ is just "the sum of the squares of all
the entries" — Pythagoras applied to the matrix flattened into one vector.
:::

We need one identity about this norm: it can be computed from the **trace** (the sum of
the diagonal entries) of $M^\top M$, and that lets us connect it to the singular values.

::: {#lem-frob-trace}
**Lemma (Frobenius norm via the trace).** For any matrix $M$,
$\|M\|_F^2 = \operatorname{tr}(M^\top M) = \sum_j \|m_j\|^2$, where $m_j$ is the $j$-th
column of $M$.
:::

::: {.proof}
The $(j,j)$ diagonal entry of $M^\top M$ is the dot product of the $j$-th column of $M$
with itself: $(M^\top M)_{jj} = \sum_{i} M_{ij}M_{ij} = \sum_i M_{ij}^2$. Summing these
diagonal entries over $j$ — which is exactly the trace — gives
$\operatorname{tr}(M^\top M) = \sum_{j}\sum_{i}M_{ij}^2$, the sum of the squares of *all*
entries, which is $\|M\|_F^2$ by @def-frob. The middle expression
$\sum_i M_{ij}^2 = \|m_j\|^2$ is the squared length of column $j$, giving the last form.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

We also need the single most useful property of the Frobenius norm: it is **unchanged by
multiplying with an orthogonal matrix.** This is what makes the SVD and the Frobenius norm
fit together perfectly.

::: {#lem-frob-orth}
**Lemma (orthogonal invariance).** If $Q$ is orthogonal ($Q^\top Q = I$) and the product
is defined, then $\|QM\|_F = \|M\|_F$ and $\|MQ\|_F = \|M\|_F$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Use @lem-frob-trace and the cyclic/transpose rules for the trace and transpose from
[GEOM-10]. For left multiplication,
$$
\|QM\|_F^2 = \operatorname{tr}\big((QM)^\top(QM)\big)
= \operatorname{tr}(M^\top Q^\top Q\, M)
= \operatorname{tr}(M^\top I\, M)
= \operatorname{tr}(M^\top M) = \|M\|_F^2 ,
$$
where the third equality is $Q^\top Q = I$ by orthogonality. Taking square roots gives
$\|QM\|_F = \|M\|_F$. For right multiplication, transpose first:
$\|MQ\|_F = \|(MQ)^\top\|_F = \|Q^\top M^\top\|_F$ — using that the Frobenius norm is
unchanged by transposing, since transposing only relabels the entries — and $Q^\top$ is
orthogonal too ($(Q^\top)^\top Q^\top = QQ^\top = I$ for square orthogonal $Q$), so the
left-multiplication result applies and gives $\|M^\top\|_F = \|M\|_F$.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Now the payoff. Apply @lem-frob-orth to the SVD itself. Because $U$ and $V$ are
orthogonal,

$$
\|A\|_F = \|U\Sigma V^\top\|_F = \|\Sigma V^\top\|_F = \|\Sigma\|_F ,
$$

(strip $U$ from the left, then $V^\top$ from the right — each is orthogonal). And $\Sigma$
is diagonal with the singular values on it, so by @def-frob,

$$
\|A\|_F^2 \;=\; \|\Sigma\|_F^2 \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sigma_i^2 .
$$ {#eq-frob-sing}

The total "energy" of a matrix — the sum of squares of all its entries — equals the sum
of the squares of its singular values. This single fact, @eq-frob-sing, drives the entire
approximation story: the energy is partitioned, cleanly and additively, among the
rank-one layers.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Where does the phrase 'variance captured' come from?"* When $A$ is a centered data
matrix (each column mean-subtracted), $\|A\|_F^2$ is proportional to the total variance in
the data, and @eq-frob-sing splits that total into per-component pieces $\sigma_i^2$. The
fraction $\sigma_k^2 / \sum_i \sigma_i^2$ is then "the share of variance the $k$-th
direction explains" — the quantity a scree plot displays. We are deriving the *algebra*
here; the *statistical* reading (centering, variance maximization, choosing $k$) is
**Unsupervised Learning's** to own, and it links back to this equation rather than
re-deriving it.
:::

## The error of a truncation, computed exactly {#sec-trunc-error}

Subtract the truncation @eq-Ak from the full sum @eq-svd-outer. The first $k$ layers
cancel, leaving exactly the *dropped* layers:

$$
A - A_k \;=\; \sum_{i=k+1}^{r}\sigma_i\, u_i v_i^\top \;=\; U\,(\Sigma - \Sigma_k)\,V^\top.
$$ {#eq-residual-matrix}

The middle matrix $\Sigma - \Sigma_k$ is diagonal with the *tail* singular values
$\sigma_{k+1},\dots,\sigma_r$ on it and zeros where the kept ones were. By the same
orthogonal-invariance argument that produced @eq-frob-sing,

$$
\|A - A_k\|_F^2 \;=\; \|\Sigma - \Sigma_k\|_F^2 \;=\; \sum_{i=k+1}^{r}\sigma_i^2 .
$$ {#eq-trunc-error}

So the squared Frobenius error of the rank-$k$ truncation is *precisely the sum of the
squares of the singular values you discarded* — nothing more, nothing approximate. If the
tail $\sigma_{k+1},\dots$ are tiny, the truncation is nearly perfect; if they are
comparable to the leaders, no rank-$k$ matrix can do well. This is already a complete,
quantitative answer for *this particular* approximation. The remaining question is whether
any *other* rank-$k$ matrix could beat it. The answer — no — is Eckart–Young.

## Eckart–Young: the truncation is optimal {#sec-eckart-young}

::: {#thm-eckart-young}
**Theorem (Eckart–Young, Frobenius norm).** Among all matrices $B$ of rank at most $k$,
the truncated SVD $A_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sigma_i u_i v_i^\top$ minimizes $\|A - B\|_F$, and
the minimum value is
$$
\min_{\operatorname{rank}(B)\le k}\|A - B\|_F \;=\; \|A - A_k\|_F
\;=\; \sqrt{\sum_{i=k+1}^{r}\sigma_i^2}\, .
$$
:::

We prove it in full. The strategy is the standard one: show that *any* rank-$\le k$
competitor $B$ must leave behind at least the tail energy. The one outside ingredient is a
fact about how singular values behave under rank constraints, which we state and use
carefully; everything else is on the page.

::: {.proof}
Let $B$ be any matrix with $\operatorname{rank}(B) \le k$. Because $B$ has rank at most
$k$, its **null space** — the set of vectors $x$ with $Bx = 0$ — has dimension at least
$n - k$. This is the rank–nullity theorem of [GEOM-12]: for an $m\times n$ matrix,
$\dim(\text{null space}) = n - \operatorname{rank} \ge n - k$.

Now consider the subspace
$W = \operatorname{span}\{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_{k+1}\}$ spanned by the **first $k+1$ right
singular vectors** of $A$. It has dimension $k+1$ (the $v_i$ are orthonormal, hence
independent). We have two subspaces of $\mathbb{R}^n$:
$\operatorname{null}(B)$ with dimension $\ge n-k$, and $W$ with dimension $k+1$. The sum
of these dimensions is at least $(n-k) + (k+1) = n+1 > n$. A standard
dimension-counting fact (the dimension of a sum of two subspaces, [GEOM-03]) is that two
subspaces of $\mathbb{R}^n$ whose dimensions add to more than $n$ must **intersect in a
nonzero vector**. So there exists a unit vector $w$ ($\|w\|=1$) lying in *both*:

$$
w \in W = \operatorname{span}\{v_1,\dots,v_{k+1}\}
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
Bw = 0 .
$$

Because $w \in W$, write it in the orthonormal $v$-basis as
$w = \sum_{i=1}^{k+1} c_i v_i$ with $\sum_i c_i^2 = \|w\|^2 = 1$ (coordinates in an
orthonormal basis are the dot products $c_i = v_i^\top w$, and Pythagoras gives the unit
sum — [GEOM-05]). Apply $A$ to $w$ using the action of the SVD on a right singular vector,
$A v_i = \sigma_i u_i$ (this is the defining relation derived in [GEOM-20]; for $i > r$
take $\sigma_i = 0$):

$$
A w \;=\; A\sum_{i=1}^{k+1} c_i v_i \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{k+1} c_i\, A v_i
\;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{k+1} c_i\,\sigma_i\, u_i .
$$

The $u_i$ are orthonormal, so the squared length of this combination is the sum of squared
coefficients (Pythagoras again):

$$
\|Aw\|^2 \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{k+1} c_i^2\,\sigma_i^2 .
$$

Now use the *ordering* $\sigma_1 \ge \dots \ge \sigma_{k+1}$: every $\sigma_i$ in this sum
is at least $\sigma_{k+1}$, so

$$
\|Aw\|^2 \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{k+1} c_i^2\,\sigma_i^2
\;\ge\; \sigma_{k+1}^2 \sum_{i=1}^{k+1} c_i^2
\;=\; \sigma_{k+1}^2 \cdot 1 \;=\; \sigma_{k+1}^2 .
$$

We have found a unit vector $w$ on which $A$ acts with length at least $\sigma_{k+1}$,
while $B$ kills it ($Bw = 0$). Therefore the difference $A - B$ does at least that much to
$w$:

$$
\|(A - B)w\| \;=\; \|Aw - Bw\| \;=\; \|Aw - 0\| \;=\; \|Aw\| \;\ge\; \sigma_{k+1} .
$$ {#eq-ey-spectral-lb}

This already proves the **spectral-norm** half of the theorem (@sec-spectral-ey below).
The spectral norm $\|M\|_2 = \max_{\|x\|=1}\|Mx\|$ is the *largest* stretch $M$ applies to
any unit vector (we use this definition in full in @sec-spectral-ey). Since a maximum over
all unit vectors is at least the value at the *particular* unit vector $w$ we just built,
@eq-ey-spectral-lb gives $\|A - B\|_2 \ge \|(A-B)w\| \ge \sigma_{k+1}$ — so the largest
stretch of $A - B$ is at least $\sigma_{k+1}$, and no rank-$k$ $B$ beats $A_k$, whose
largest leftover stretch is exactly $\sigma_{k+1}$.

For the **Frobenius** statement we need the stronger fact that $B$ cannot beat $A_k$ on
the *sum* of squared leftover singular values, not just the single largest. The clean way
is the following inequality on singular values of a sum, **Weyl's inequality**, which we
state as the imported ingredient and then apply:

> For any matrices $X, Y$ and indices $i, j \ge 1$ with $i + j - 1 \le \min(m,n)$,
> $\sigma_{i+j-1}(X + Y) \le \sigma_i(X) + \sigma_j(Y)$, where $\sigma_\ell(\cdot)$ denotes
> the $\ell$-th largest singular value. (This follows from the min–max characterization of
> singular values; it is the singular-value analogue of the eigenvalue interlacing used in
> [GEOM-16], and we flag it as the one fact carried in rather than rebuilt here.)

Apply it with $X = A - B$, $Y = B$, $j = k+1$, and any $i \ge 1$. Since
$\operatorname{rank}(B)\le k$, its $(k+1)$-th singular value is zero:
$\sigma_{k+1}(B) = 0$. Weyl gives

$$
\sigma_{i + k}(\underbrace{(A-B) + B}_{=\,A})
\;\le\; \sigma_i(A - B) + \sigma_{k+1}(B)
\;=\; \sigma_i(A - B) + 0 ,
$$

that is, $\sigma_i(A - B) \ge \sigma_{i+k}(A) = \sigma_{i+k}$ for every $i \ge 1$. Summing
the squares over $i = 1, 2, \dots$ and reindexing with $\ell = i + k$,

$$
\|A - B\|_F^2 \;=\; \sum_{i\ge 1}\sigma_i(A-B)^2
\;\ge\; \sum_{i\ge 1}\sigma_{i+k}^2
\;=\; \sum_{\ell = k+1}^{r}\sigma_\ell^2
\;=\; \|A - A_k\|_F^2 ,
$$

where the first equality is the Frobenius-norm-from-singular-values identity
@eq-frob-sing applied to $A - B$, and the last equality is the exact truncation error
@eq-trunc-error. Thus $\|A - B\|_F \ge \|A - A_k\|_F$ for every rank-$\le k$ matrix $B$,
and $A_k$ itself attains the bound. The minimizer is $A_k$ and the minimum is
$\sqrt{\sum_{i>k}\sigma_i^2}$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

::: {.callout-note title="What we borrowed, and where it is built"}
This proof imported two facts and flagged each. The **rank–nullity** dimension count and
the **two-subspaces-must-intersect** lemma are built in [GEOM-12] and [GEOM-03]
respectively. **Weyl's inequality** for singular values is the one genuinely new
ingredient; it is the singular-value cousin of the eigenvalue min–max / interlacing
results that the spectral theorem development ([GEOM-16]) sets up. We used it as a stated
tool so the Frobenius argument could run end to end without a multi-page detour into the
min–max theory. The *spectral-norm* half, by contrast, we proved completely from scratch
in @eq-ey-spectral-lb.
:::

## The spectral-norm version, and why both norms agree on the answer {#sec-spectral-ey}

The **spectral norm** (or operator 2-norm) measures the *largest stretch* a matrix
applies to any unit vector:
$\|M\|_2 = \max_{\|x\|=1}\|Mx\|$. As [GEOM-20] established, this maximum is achieved along
the top right singular vector and equals the largest singular value: $\|M\|_2 = \sigma_1(M)$.

The leftover matrix $A - A_k = \sum_{i>k}\sigma_i u_i v_i^\top$ is itself an SVD (its
singular values are $\sigma_{k+1}\ge\sigma_{k+2}\ge\dots$), so its largest is
$\sigma_{k+1}$:

$$
\|A - A_k\|_2 \;=\; \sigma_{k+1} .
$$

And @eq-ey-spectral-lb showed every rank-$\le k$ competitor has
$\|A - B\|_2 \ge \sigma_{k+1}$. Hence:

::: {#thm-eckart-young-2}
**Theorem (Eckart–Young, spectral norm).** Among all matrices $B$ of rank at most $k$,
$A_k$ minimizes $\|A - B\|_2$, with minimum value $\sigma_{k+1}$.
:::

The remarkable thing — and a favorite exam point — is that the **same** matrix $A_k$ is
optimal for *both* norms at once. The Frobenius norm cares about total leftover energy
$\sqrt{\sum_{i>k}\sigma_i^2}$; the spectral norm cares only about the largest leftover
stretch $\sigma_{k+1}$; and the truncated SVD minimizes both simultaneously. That is not a
coincidence — it is because both norms are *unitarily invariant* functions of the singular
values, and truncation is the unique way to make the leftover singular values as small as
possible in the strong, term-by-term sense the proof exposed.

## Worked example 1 — image compression (an honest one) {#sec-image}

The cleanest demonstration of Eckart–Young is image compression, because the "matrix" is
literally a picture you can look at. A grayscale image *is* a matrix: entry $(i,j)$ is the
brightness of pixel $(i,j)$. We build a synthetic but realistic image with genuine
low-rank structure (smooth gradients plus a few sharp features) and watch the rank-$k$
truncations sharpen as $k$ grows, alongside the exact error predicted by
@eq-trunc-error.

```{python}
#| label: fig-image
#| fig-cap: 'Eckart–Young on a $200\times 200$ grayscale image. The original (top-left) is reconstructed at ranks $k = 2, 5, 20, 60$. The rank-2 version captures the gross light/dark layout; by rank 20 the structure is essentially recovered; rank 60 is visually indistinguishable. Each panel''s title reports the *measured* relative Frobenius error and — in parentheses — the value $\sqrt{\sum_{i>k}\sigma_i^2}/\|A\|_F$ predicted by @eq-trunc-error. They match to plotting precision: the theorem is exact, not approximate.'

rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
N = 200
xx, yy = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(-3, 3, N), np.linspace(-3, 3, N))
# smooth low-rank-ish structure + a couple of sharp blobs + faint noise
img = (np.exp(-(xx**2 + yy**2)/4)
       + 0.6*np.sin(0.8*xx)*np.cos(0.6*yy)
       + 0.4*np.exp(-((xx-1.5)**2 + (yy+1.0)**2)*3)
       + 0.02*rng.standard_normal((N, N)))
img = (img - img.min())/(img.max() - img.min())

U, s, Vt = np.linalg.svd(img, full_matrices=False)
total_energy = np.sum(s**2)

def rank_k(k):
    return (U[:, :k] * s[:k]) @ Vt[:k, :]

ks = [2, 5, 20, 60]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 5, figsize=(10.5, 2.6))
axes[0].imshow(img, cmap="gray", vmin=0, vmax=1)
axes[0].set_title(f"original\nrank {len(s)}", fontsize=9)
for ax, k in zip(axes[1:], ks):
    Ak = rank_k(k)
    measured = np.linalg.norm(img - Ak, 'fro')/np.linalg.norm(img, 'fro')
    predicted = np.sqrt(np.sum(s[k:]**2))/np.sqrt(total_energy)
    ax.imshow(Ak, cmap="gray", vmin=0, vmax=1)
    ax.set_title(f"k={k}\nerr {measured:.3f}\n({predicted:.3f})", fontsize=9)
for ax in axes:
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
plt.tight_layout()
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-scree
#| fig-cap: 'The singular-value spectrum of the same image (left, log scale) and the cumulative energy fraction $\sum_{i\le k}\sigma_i^2 / \sum_i \sigma_i^2$ (right). The spectrum falls off fast — the image''s information is concentrated in the first dozen-or-so directions — which is *why* low-rank approximation works here. The horizontal line marks $99\%$ of the energy; reaching it takes only a small fraction of the full rank. A flat spectrum (no such decay) would mean truncation buys nothing — the honest precondition for this whole technique.'

cum = np.cumsum(s**2)/total_energy
k99 = int(np.searchsorted(cum, 0.99)) + 1

fig, (a0, a1) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.4))
a0.semilogy(np.arange(1, len(s)+1), s, color=VEC, marker=".", ms=4, lw=1.2)
a0.set_xlabel("index $i$"); a0.set_ylabel("$\\sigma_i$ (log)")
a0.set_title("Singular-value spectrum")
a0.set_xlim(0, 60)

a1.plot(np.arange(1, len(s)+1), cum, color=VEC, lw=2.0)
a1.axhline(0.99, color=HILITE, ls="--", lw=1.4, label="99% energy")
a1.axvline(k99, color=ACCENT, ls=":", lw=1.4, label=f"k={k99}")
a1.set_xlabel("rank $k$"); a1.set_ylabel("cumulative energy fraction")
a1.set_title("Energy captured vs. rank"); a1.set_xlim(0, 60)
a1.legend(fontsize=9, loc="lower right")
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"reaching 99% of the energy takes rank k = {k99} out of {len(s)}")
```

The parenthesized prediction and the measured error agree in @fig-image because
@eq-trunc-error is an identity, not a heuristic. And @fig-scree shows the precondition for
the whole enterprise: the singular values must *decay*. When they do — as in natural
images, correlated feature tables, and recommendation matrices — a low rank captures
nearly everything. When they do not, there is no good low-rank approximation to be had,
and Eckart–Young honestly tells you so by reporting a large leftover energy. We meet such
a case next.

## Worked example 2 — the edge of applicability: a near-full-rank matrix {#sec-edge}

The danger with low-rank approximation is assuming every matrix *has* useful low-rank
structure. It does not. A matrix whose singular values are all comparable — for instance,
nearly isotropic noise, or a permutation-like structure with no dominant directions —
cannot be compressed without serious loss, and truncation will *throw away real signal*.
This is the honest boundary. @fig-edge contrasts a low-rank-friendly matrix with a
near-full-rank one and shows what rank-$k$ truncation costs in each.

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge
#| fig-cap: 'When low-rank approximation fails. **Left:** singular-value spectra of two $80\times 80$ matrices — one with fast decay (blue, compressible) and one that is essentially full-rank random noise (red, flat). **Right:** relative Frobenius error of the rank-$k$ truncation versus $k$. For the compressible matrix the error plunges; for the flat-spectrum matrix it falls only as fast as $\sqrt{1 - k/r}$ — you must keep almost every component to lose little, so truncation buys nothing. The flat spectrum is the precise signature of a matrix you should *not* try to compress.'

n = 80
rng = np.random.default_rng(11)

# compressible: a few strong directions + tiny noise
Lc = rng.standard_normal((n, 4)); Rc = rng.standard_normal((4, n))
M_good = Lc @ Rc + 0.01*rng.standard_normal((n, n))

# near-full-rank: isotropic noise, all singular values comparable
M_bad = rng.standard_normal((n, n))

sg = np.linalg.svd(M_good, compute_uv=False)
sb = np.linalg.svd(M_bad, compute_uv=False)

def err_curve(s):
    total = np.sum(s**2)
    out = []
    for k in range(len(s)+1):
        out.append(np.sqrt(np.sum(s[k:]**2)/total))
    return np.array(out)

eg, eb = err_curve(sg), err_curve(sb)

fig, (a0, a1) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.4))
a0.semilogy(np.arange(1, n+1), sg/sg[0], color=VEC, lw=1.8, label="compressible")
a0.semilogy(np.arange(1, n+1), sb/sb[0], color=HILITE, lw=1.8, label="full-rank noise")
a0.set_xlabel("index $i$"); a0.set_ylabel("$\\sigma_i/\\sigma_1$ (log)")
a0.set_title("Spectra: decaying vs. flat"); a0.legend(fontsize=9)

a1.plot(np.arange(n+1), eg, color=VEC, lw=2.0, label="compressible")
a1.plot(np.arange(n+1), eb, color=HILITE, lw=2.0, label="full-rank noise")
a1.set_xlabel("rank $k$"); a1.set_ylabel("relative Frobenius error")
a1.set_title("Truncation error vs. rank"); a1.legend(fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"rank-10 error  compressible: {eg[10]:.3f}   full-rank noise: {eb[10]:.3f}")
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**A low-rank approximation is only as good as the singular-value decay.** Eckart–Young
guarantees you the *best* rank-$k$ matrix — it does **not** guarantee that the best
rank-$k$ matrix is any good. Always look at the spectrum (the scree plot) before
truncating. If the singular values plateau, there is no low-dimensional structure to find;
truncating then discards genuine signal and the "compression" is just damage. The right
reaction to a flat spectrum is to *not* use low-rank approximation, or to question whether
the matrix is the right representation of your problem at all.
:::

# Part II — The Pseudoinverse {#sec-pinv}

We turn to the second face of the Core. The goal is a single object $A^+$ that "solves"
$Ax = b$ in every case — tall, wide, or rank-deficient — and reduces to $A^{-1}$ when the
ordinary inverse exists. The SVD builds it almost mechanically.

## Building $A^+$ by inverting the layers {#sec-build-pinv}

Start from $A = U\Sigma V^\top$. If $A$ were square and invertible, every singular value
would be positive and we could invert each factor: since $U,V$ are orthogonal their
inverses are their transposes ($U^{-1}=U^\top$, $V^{-1}=V^\top$, from [GEOM-05]), and
$\Sigma$ is diagonal so its inverse just reciprocates the diagonal. Using
$(XYZ)^{-1} = Z^{-1}Y^{-1}X^{-1}$ from [GEOM-11],

$$
A^{-1} = (U\Sigma V^\top)^{-1} = (V^\top)^{-1}\Sigma^{-1}U^{-1} = V\,\Sigma^{-1}U^\top .
$$

The obstruction in the general case is only $\Sigma^{-1}$: when $A$ is rectangular or
rank-deficient, some diagonal entries of $\Sigma$ are zero (or $\Sigma$ is not even
square), and we cannot reciprocate a zero. The pseudoinverse makes exactly *one* edit —
the only sensible one — and leaves the orthogonal factors alone.

::: {#def-pinv}
**Definition (Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse).** Let $A = U\Sigma V^\top$ be the SVD of an
$m\times n$ matrix of rank $r$. Define the $n\times m$ diagonal matrix $\Sigma^+$ by
**reciprocating the nonzero singular values and transposing the shape**:
$$
(\Sigma^+)_{ii} = \begin{cases} 1/\sigma_i, & \sigma_i > 0,\\[2pt] 0, & \sigma_i = 0,\end{cases}
\qquad i = 1,\dots,r, \quad\text{rest } = 0 .
$$
Then the **pseudoinverse** is
$$
A^+ \;=\; V\,\Sigma^+\,U^\top \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{r}\frac{1}{\sigma_i}\,v_i u_i^\top .
$$
:::

The defining choice is the second line of the case split: **a zero singular value maps to
zero, not to infinity.** That is the whole content of "the inverse that knows which
directions it is allowed to touch" from the intuition. On the directions $A$ actually
stretches ($\sigma_i > 0$), $A^+$ un-stretches by $1/\sigma_i$; on the directions $A$
collapses ($\sigma_i = 0$), $A^+$ does nothing rather than blowing up.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is the pseudoinverse really a generalization of the inverse — does it agree when $A$ is
invertible?"* Yes, and it falls right out. If $A$ is square with full rank $r = n$, every
$\sigma_i > 0$, so $\Sigma^+ = \Sigma^{-1}$ exactly, and
$A^+ = V\Sigma^{-1}U^\top = A^{-1}$ by the inversion above. The case split never fires.
So $A^+$ is a strict extension: identical to $A^{-1}$ where the latter exists, and defined
everywhere else too. Penrose's original 1955 paper defines $A^+$ axiomatically by four
equations ($AA^+A = A$, $A^+AA^+ = A^+$, and $(AA^+)$, $(A^+A)$ symmetric) and proves it
unique; the SVD construction here is the same object, and you can verify all four
properties directly from the definition.
:::

## What $A^+ b$ actually does: the geometry {#sec-pinv-geometry}

The payoff is understanding $\hat x = A^+ b$ geometrically. Decompose using the SVD pieces.
Write $b$ in the orthonormal $u$-basis of $\mathbb{R}^m$: $b = \sum_i (u_i^\top b)\,u_i$.
Then

$$
A^+ b \;=\; \Big(\sum_{i=1}^{r}\frac{1}{\sigma_i}v_i u_i^\top\Big) b
\;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{r}\frac{u_i^\top b}{\sigma_i}\,v_i .
$$ {#eq-pinv-action}

Read this carefully against the two failure cases from @sec-why-noinverse.

**The over-determined / least-squares face.** The sum in @eq-pinv-action runs only to
$r$, the rank. Any component of $b$ along $u_{r+1},\dots,u_m$ — directions *outside* the
column space of $A$, the part of $b$ that $A$ can never reach — is simply ignored
($u_i^\top b$ for $i>r$ never appears). That is exactly the orthogonal projection of
[GEOM-06]: $A\hat x = AA^+b$ is the projection of $b$ onto the column space of $A$, the
closest reachable point, and $\hat x$ is the least-squares solution. The pseudoinverse
*automatically* discards the unreachable part of $b$.

**The under-determined / minimum-norm face.** When there are many exact solutions, they
differ by anything in the null space of $A$ (add a null-space vector to a solution and
$A$ maps it to the same $b$). The output @eq-pinv-action is built purely from
$v_1,\dots,v_r$ — the **row space** of $A$, which is orthogonal to the null space
([GEOM-12]). So $A^+b$ contains **no** null-space component. Among all exact solutions, the
one with no null-space part is the **shortest** (any added null-space piece only lengthens
it, by Pythagoras). The pseudoinverse *automatically* picks the minimum-norm solution.

We now prove the least-squares-and-minimum-norm claim as a theorem, every step shown.

::: {#thm-pinv-mn}
**Theorem (pseudoinverse = minimum-norm least squares).** For any $A$ ($m\times n$, rank
$r$) and any $b\in\mathbb{R}^m$, the vector $\hat x = A^+ b$ is the unique solution of
$$
\min_{x}\ \|Ax - b\|^2 ,
$$
and among all minimizers it is the one of **smallest norm** $\|x\|$.
:::

::: {.proof}
**Step 1 — change to the singular coordinate systems.** Because $U$ and $V$ are
orthogonal, multiplying by them does not change lengths (orthogonal maps preserve norm,
[GEOM-05]): $\|U^\top z\| = \|z\|$ for any $z$. So rewrite the residual norm using
$A = U\Sigma V^\top$ and insert $U U^\top = I$:

$$
\|Ax - b\|
= \|U\Sigma V^\top x - b\|
= \|U(\Sigma V^\top x - U^\top b)\|
= \|\Sigma V^\top x - U^\top b\| ,
$$

where the last equality strips the orthogonal $U$. Introduce new coordinates
$z = V^\top x$ (so $x = Vz$, and $\|x\| = \|z\|$ since $V$ is orthogonal) and the
transformed target $c = U^\top b$. The problem becomes, transparently,

$$
\min_{z}\ \|\Sigma z - c\|^2 ,
$$

with $\Sigma$ diagonal. We have turned a coupled least-squares problem into a
**decoupled, scalar** one — the entire reason the SVD makes this clean.

**Step 2 — solve the decoupled problem coordinate by coordinate.** Because $\Sigma$ is
diagonal, $\Sigma z$ has $i$-th entry $\sigma_i z_i$ for $i \le r$ and $0$ for the rest
(those rows of $\Sigma$ are zero, since $\sigma_i = 0$ there). So

$$
\|\Sigma z - c\|^2
= \sum_{i=1}^{r}\big(\sigma_i z_i - c_i\big)^2
\;+\; \sum_{i=r+1}^{m} c_i^2 .
$$

The second sum, $\sum_{i>r}c_i^2$, contains **no $z$** at all — it is the squared length
of the part of $b$ outside the column space, an *irreducible* error we cannot touch by any
choice of $x$. (This is precisely the unreachable component from @sec-pinv-geometry.) The
first sum is minimized term by term: each $(\sigma_i z_i - c_i)^2$ with $\sigma_i > 0$ is
driven to **zero** by the unique choice

$$
z_i = \frac{c_i}{\sigma_i}, \qquad i = 1,\dots,r .
$$

For indices $i > r$ within $z$ (the directions $A$ collapses, $v_{r+1},\dots,v_n$), the
variable $z_i$ does **not appear** in the residual at all — it is free. So the residual is
minimized by *any* choice of those free coordinates; the least-squares fit does not pin
them down. This is the multiplicity of solutions in the rank-deficient / wide case, seen
in coordinates.

**Step 3 — among all minimizers, choose the shortest.** The minimizing $z$ has its first
$r$ coordinates fixed at $c_i/\sigma_i$ and its remaining coordinates $z_{r+1},\dots,z_n$
free. Its squared length is

$$
\|z\|^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{r}\Big(\frac{c_i}{\sigma_i}\Big)^2
\;+\; \sum_{i=r+1}^{n} z_i^2 .
$$

The first sum is fixed; the second is a sum of squares we are free to choose, and it is
minimized — to zero — by setting every free coordinate to zero. So the **minimum-norm**
minimizer is

$$
z_i = \begin{cases} c_i/\sigma_i, & i \le r,\\ 0, & i > r.\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — translate back.** This is exactly $z = \Sigma^+ c$ by @def-pinv (reciprocate
the nonzero $\sigma$'s, zero elsewhere). Undo the coordinate change with $x = Vz$ and
$c = U^\top b$:

$$
\hat x = V z = V\Sigma^+ c = V\Sigma^+ U^\top b = A^+ b .
$$

So $A^+ b$ minimizes the residual (Step 2) and, among all such minimizers, has the
smallest norm (Step 3) — and it is unique because the fixed coordinates are forced and the
free ones are pinned to zero. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This proof is the heart of Part II. Every claim made loosely in @sec-pinv-geometry —
"ignores the unreachable part of $b$," "picks the shortest solution," "reduces to least
squares" — fell out as a labeled step. @fig-pinv-cases draws the two regimes.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pinv-cases
#| fig-cap: 'The two regimes the pseudoinverse handles, in the plane. **Left (over-determined):** three noisy points constrain a line through the origin, $y = x\,\theta$, with no exact fit; $\hat\theta = A^+ y$ is the least-squares slope, and $A\hat\theta$ is the orthogonal projection of $y$ onto the column space (the reachable line). **Right (under-determined):** a single equation $a^\top x = c$ in two unknowns has a whole line of exact solutions (grey); $A^+ c$ (green) is the one closest to the origin — the foot of the perpendicular from the origin to that solution line, the minimum-norm choice.'

# Left: over-determined least squares (1 unknown slope, 3 equations)
A_over = np.array([[1.0], [2.0], [3.0]])
y_over = np.array([1.1, 1.7, 3.4])
theta = float((np.linalg.pinv(A_over) @ y_over)[0])

# Right: under-determined, a^T x = c, line of solutions
a = np.array([1.0, 2.0]); c = 2.0
x_mn = np.linalg.pinv(a.reshape(1, -1)) @ np.array([c])  # minimum-norm
# parametrize the solution line: x_mn + t * (null direction)
nulldir = np.array([-a[1], a[0]]); nulldir = nulldir/np.linalg.norm(nulldir)
ts = np.linspace(-2.2, 2.2, 50)
sol_line = x_mn[:, None] + nulldir[:, None]*ts

fig, (a0, a1) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.6))

xs = np.linspace(0, 3.4, 50)
a0.scatter(A_over.ravel(), y_over, color=VEC, s=45, zorder=3, label="data")
a0.plot(xs, theta*xs, color=HILITE, lw=2.0, label=f"LS fit  $\\hat\\theta={theta:.3f}$")
for xi, yi in zip(A_over.ravel(), y_over):
    a0.plot([xi, xi], [yi, theta*xi], color=GREY, lw=1.2)
a0.set_title("Over-determined: least squares")
a0.set_xlabel("$x$"); a0.set_ylabel("$y$"); a0.legend(fontsize=8, loc="upper left")

a1.plot(sol_line[0], sol_line[1], color=GREY, lw=2.0, label="all solutions $a^\\top x=c$")
draw_vec(a1, (0, 0), tuple(x_mn), color=ACCENT, label="$A^+c$ (min-norm)", label_offset=(0.15, -0.35))
a1.scatter([0], [0], color=VEC, s=40, zorder=4); a1.text(0.05, 0.12, "origin", fontsize=9, color=VEC)
a1.set_title("Under-determined: minimum norm")
a1.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); a1.set_ylabel("$x_2$"); a1.set_aspect("equal")
a1.legend(fontsize=8, loc="upper right"); a1.set_xlim(-1.5, 2.0); a1.set_ylim(-1.0, 2.2)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"over-determined LS slope  = {theta:.4f}")
print(f"under-determined min-norm = ({x_mn[0]:.4f}, {x_mn[1]:.4f}),  |x| = {np.linalg.norm(x_mn):.4f}")
```

## Worked example — rank-deficient regression, where the inverse dies {#sec-rankdef}

Now the case that motivated the whole construction: a regression design where two
predictors are perfectly collinear, so $A^\top A$ is singular and the textbook
$(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top y$ is undefined. The pseudoinverse sails through, returning the
minimum-norm coefficient vector. This is the linear-algebra service Geometry hands to
Regression for [REGR-05] and [REGR-14].

```{python}
#| label: tbl-rankdef
#| echo: false
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n_obs = 40
x1 = rng.uniform(0, 10, n_obs)
x2 = 3.0 + 2.0*x1                      # x2 is an EXACT linear function of x1
x3 = rng.uniform(0, 5, n_obs)         # a genuinely independent predictor
A = np.column_stack([np.ones(n_obs), x1, x2, x3])  # design with intercept
beta_true = np.array([1.0, 0.5, 0.0, -0.8])
y = A @ beta_true + rng.normal(0, 0.4, n_obs)

AtA = A.T @ A
rank_A = np.linalg.matrix_rank(A)
print(f"design A is {A.shape[0]} x {A.shape[1]},  rank = {rank_A}  (deficient: x2 = 3 + 2*x1)")
print(f"A^T A is {AtA.shape[0]} x {AtA.shape[1]},  its rank = {np.linalg.matrix_rank(AtA)}  -> SINGULAR")
print(f"condition number of A^T A = {np.linalg.cond(AtA):.3e}  (effectively infinite)")

# the normal-equations formula is undefined: A^T A has no true inverse, so
# np.linalg.inv returns an arbitrary, unstable answer instead of refusing.
beta_ne = np.linalg.inv(AtA) @ A.T @ y
print(f"\n(A^T A)^-1 A^T y returns:        {np.round(beta_ne, 3)}")
print(f"  it does not even fit as well:   residual = {np.linalg.norm(A @ beta_ne - y):.4f}")
# perturb A^T A by a numerically negligible amount and the answer changes completely:
beta_ne2 = np.linalg.inv(AtA + 1e-10*np.eye(4)) @ A.T @ y
print(f"  perturb A^T A by 1e-10:          {np.round(beta_ne2, 3)}   <- totally different")

# pseudoinverse succeeds, returns the minimum-norm least-squares fit:
beta_pinv = np.linalg.pinv(A) @ y
print(f"\npseudoinverse (min-norm LS):     {np.round(beta_pinv, 3)}")
print(f"  best possible fit:              residual = {np.linalg.norm(A @ beta_pinv - y):.4f}")
print(f"  smallest norm among LS fits:    ||beta|| = {np.linalg.norm(beta_pinv):.4f}")
```

The matrix $A^\top A$ is singular — its condition number is effectively infinite — because
the collinear columns $x_1$ and $x_2$ give the design rank $3$ instead of $4$. The
textbook formula $(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top y$ does not refuse outright; worse, it silently
returns an *arbitrary* answer — one that does not even achieve the smallest residual, and
that swings to a completely different vector when $A^\top A$ is perturbed by a numerically
negligible $10^{-10}$. That instability is the floating-point face of "the inverse does not
exist." The pseudoinverse instead returns the genuine best-fitting line (smallest residual)
while distributing the coefficient between the two collinear predictors in the unique
**minimum-norm** way. @fig-rankdef makes the indeterminacy visible: the
*fit* is unique even though the *coefficients* are not.

```{python}
#| label: fig-rankdef
#| fig-cap: 'Rank-deficient regression. **Left:** fitted vs. observed $y$ — the pseudoinverse fit is excellent ($45^\circ$ line), because the *predictions* are well-determined even when the coefficients are not. **Right:** the coefficient indeterminacy. Any split of the combined effect between the collinear $x_1$ and $x_2$ along the grey line gives the *same* fit; the pseudoinverse picks the green point, the one closest to the origin (minimum norm). This is the geometric meaning of ''the pseudoinverse chooses for you'' — and a warning that the individual collinear coefficients are not interpretable.'

fitted = A @ beta_pinv
fig, (a0, a1) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.6))

lim = [y.min()-0.5, y.max()+0.5]
a0.scatter(y, fitted, color=VEC, s=30, zorder=3)
a0.plot(lim, lim, color=HILITE, lw=1.6, ls="--", label="perfect fit")
a0.set_xlabel("observed $y$"); a0.set_ylabel("fitted $\\hat y$")
a0.set_title("Predictions are well-determined"); a0.legend(fontsize=9, loc="upper left")
a0.set_xlim(lim); a0.set_ylim(lim)

# the family of equivalent (beta1, beta2) splits: beta1 + 2*beta2 = const
b1p, b2p = beta_pinv[1], beta_pinv[2]
combined = b1p + 2.0*b2p
b2_grid = np.linspace(-1.5, 1.5, 50)
b1_grid = combined - 2.0*b2_grid
a1.plot(b1_grid, b2_grid, color=GREY, lw=2.0, label="all equivalent splits")
a1.scatter([b1p], [b2p], color=ACCENT, s=80, zorder=4, edgecolor="white", label="pseudoinverse (min-norm)")
a1.scatter([0], [0], color=VEC, s=30, zorder=3); a1.text(0.05, 0.08, "origin", fontsize=9, color=VEC)
a1.set_xlabel("$\\beta_1$ (coef of $x_1$)"); a1.set_ylabel("$\\beta_2$ (coef of $x_2$)")
a1.set_title("Coefficients are NOT"); a1.legend(fontsize=8, loc="upper right"); a1.set_aspect("equal")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**A small singular value is a loaded gun.** The pseudoinverse divides by each kept
$\sigma_i$, so a singular value that is *nonzero but tiny* contributes a factor
$1/\sigma_i$ that is *huge*. In exact arithmetic $\sigma_i > 0$ means "invert it," but in
floating point a $\sigma_i$ near the noise floor is indistinguishable from zero and
dividing by it amplifies measurement noise catastrophically — the very instability the
condition number $\kappa = \sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}$ measures ([GEOM-26]). The practical
fix is the **truncated** pseudoinverse: treat singular values below a tolerance as zero
(NumPy's `pinv` does this via its `rcond` argument), trading a little bias for a lot of
stability. This is the exact same dividing line as Part I — and it is *also* what ridge
regression ([REGR-14]) does smoothly, by replacing $1/\sigma_i$ with
$\sigma_i/(\sigma_i^2 + \lambda)$, which stays bounded as $\sigma_i \to 0$.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When should I reach for the pseudoinverse, and when for something else?"* The honest
decision tree:

- **Full column rank, well-conditioned, tall $A$:** you want plain least squares. Use a
  **QR factorization** ([GEOM-07]) — it is faster than the SVD and numerically sound. The
  pseudoinverse gives the same answer but does more work.
- **Rank-deficient or genuinely ill-conditioned $A$:** use the **SVD/pseudoinverse**, and
  truncate the small singular values. This is its home turf — it is the only method that
  degrades gracefully instead of exploding.
- **You want to *regularize* rather than truncate:** use **ridge** ([REGR-14]). It is the
  smooth cousin of pseudoinverse truncation and usually predicts better when collinearity
  is a matter of degree rather than exact redundancy.
- **Never** form $A^\top A$ and invert it when $A$ is ill-conditioned: that *squares* the
  condition number ([GEOM-26]) and destroys precision. The whole reason QR and SVD exist
  in numerical practice is to avoid that squaring.

And the conceptual catch an interviewer will probe: *the pseudoinverse makes the
prediction unique, not the coefficients.* In the collinear case the individual
coefficients are an arbitrary (minimum-norm) choice and must not be interpreted as
"effects."
:::

# How these two halves are one idea {#sec-unity}

It is worth pausing on why Parts I and II belong in the same article. Both are "use the
big singular values, discard the small ones," and the picture in @fig-summary makes the
symmetry exact.

- **Low-rank approximation** replaces the singular value map $\sigma_i \mapsto \sigma_i$
  with the truncation $\sigma_i \mapsto \sigma_i\cdot\mathbf{1}[i\le k]$: keep the big ones
  at full strength, zero the small ones. The result is $A_k$.
- **The pseudoinverse** replaces $\sigma_i \mapsto 1/\sigma_i$ with the truncated reciprocal
  $\sigma_i \mapsto (1/\sigma_i)\cdot\mathbf{1}[\sigma_i > \tau]$: invert the big ones, zero
  the small ones. The result is $A^+$ (truncated).

In both, the SVD has done the essential work of *diagonalizing the matrix into independent
directions ranked by importance*, so that we can make a clean per-direction decision —
keep or discard — that would be hopeless on the entangled entries of $A$ itself. That is
the deepest reason the SVD is called the master decomposition: it turns every hard matrix
question into an easy decision, one singular value at a time.

```{python}
#| label: fig-summary
#| fig-cap: 'The two operations as transformations of the singular values, on a shared spectrum. **Top:** the raw singular values $\sigma_i$ (blue), with the cutoff $k$ marked. **Bottom-left:** low-rank approximation keeps $\sigma_i$ for $i\le k$ and zeros the rest. **Bottom-right:** the pseudoinverse maps each kept $\sigma_i$ to $1/\sigma_i$ and zeros the rest — note how the *smallest* kept singular value becomes the *largest* reciprocal, which is why a small surviving $\sigma$ is dangerous. Same cutoff, two mirror-image edits.'

s_demo = np.array([8.0, 5.5, 3.2, 2.0, 1.1, 0.6, 0.25, 0.08])
k = 4
idx = np.arange(1, len(s_demo)+1)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(10.2, 3.2))
a0, a1, a2 = axes
a0.bar(idx, s_demo, color=VEC)
a0.axvline(k+0.5, color=HILITE, ls="--", lw=1.4)
a0.set_title("raw $\\sigma_i$"); a0.set_xlabel("index $i$")

trunc = s_demo.copy(); trunc[k:] = 0
a1.bar(idx, trunc, color=ACCENT)
a1.axvline(k+0.5, color=HILITE, ls="--", lw=1.4)
a1.set_title("low-rank: keep big, zero small"); a1.set_xlabel("index $i$")

inv = np.where(idx <= k, 1.0/s_demo, 0.0)
a2.bar(idx, inv, color=HILITE)
a2.axvline(k+0.5, color=HILITE, ls="--", lw=1.4)
a2.set_title("pseudoinverse: $1/\\sigma_i$ on kept"); a2.set_xlabel("index $i$")
plt.tight_layout()
```

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

Collecting the honest boundaries scattered above, plus one more:

- **Low rank only helps if the spectrum decays.** A flat singular-value spectrum
  (@sec-edge) means there is no low-dimensional structure; truncation then destroys signal.
  *Reach for:* nothing — accept the full rank, or change the representation.
- **The pseudoinverse is unstable near rank-deficiency.** Tiny nonzero singular values
  produce enormous $1/\sigma_i$ factors that amplify noise (@sec-rankdef pitfall).
  *Reach for:* truncated SVD (set a tolerance) or ridge regularization ([REGR-14]).
- **Both norms in Eckart–Young assume you measure error the natural way.** If your problem
  weights entries unequally (some matrix entries matter more, or are missing), plain
  Frobenius/spectral optimality no longer applies. *Reach for:* weighted or masked
  low-rank methods — matrix completion, weighted PCA — which the unconstrained SVD does
  *not* solve. (This is exactly why Netflix-style recommendation, with most entries
  missing, needs more than a raw SVD truncation.)
- **The SVD costs $O(mn\min(m,n))$.** For very large sparse matrices, a full SVD is
  infeasible; you compute only the top $k$ singular triples (randomized or Krylov methods).
  The *theory* here is unchanged — Eckart–Young still names the optimum — but the
  *computation* must be truncated from the start.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is the truncated SVD the same as PCA?"* They are intimately related but not identical,
and the distinction is the Geometry/Unsupervised-Learning seam. PCA is the SVD applied to
a **centered** data matrix (each feature mean-subtracted); its principal components are the
right singular vectors $v_i$, and the variance along component $i$ is $\sigma_i^2/(n-1)$.
The truncated SVD is the *algebra* — best rank-$k$ approximation of *whatever* matrix you
hand it. PCA is the *statistics* — best low-dimensional summary of *centered data*, with
the variance interpretation. If you forget to center, you are doing truncated SVD, not
PCA, and the leading component will partly capture the mean rather than the variation. The
centering, the covariance interpretation, scree plots, and choosing $k$ are **Unsupervised
Learning's** to own; this article supplies the $A = U\Sigma V^\top$ machinery they build
on.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[GEOM-20 *The Singular Value Decomposition*]**, which proved that every
matrix factors as $A = U\Sigma V^\top$ and read its geometry as rotate–stretch–rotate.
This article cashed that factorization into the two tools the rest of the series leans on:

- **Eckart–Young low-rank approximation** — handed forward as the linear-algebra core of
  **PCA** in **Unsupervised Learning**, which adds the centering and variance story and
  links back to @thm-eckart-young rather than re-deriving it.
- **The Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse** — handed to **Regression** for **rank-deficient and
  high-dimensional designs** ([REGR-05] multiple regression, [REGR-14] ridge), completing
  the least-squares-as-projection picture of [GEOM-06] to the cases where $A^\top A$ is
  singular.

The trunk of Geometry now turns to **calculus on vectors**: [GEOM-22] builds vector and
matrix differentiation from the Calc-1 floor, and the stability theme touched here — small
singular values, the danger of dividing by them — gets its own article in **[GEOM-26
*Condition Number and Numerical Stability*]**, where $\kappa = \sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}$
is derived as the worst-case error amplification and ties this article's pitfalls to
multicollinearity ([REGR-07]).
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary sources.** C. Eckart and G. Young, "The approximation of one matrix by another
  of lower rank," *Psychometrika* 1 (1936) — the optimality theorem of @sec-eckart-young,
  proved in the Frobenius norm; L. Mirsky (1960) extended it to all unitarily invariant
  norms (including the spectral norm of @sec-spectral-ey). R. Penrose, "A generalized
  inverse for matrices," *Proc. Cambridge Phil. Soc.* 51 (1955) — the four-axiom definition
  of $A^+$ and its uniqueness; E. H. Moore announced the same object in 1920.
- **The numerical companion.** G. H. Golub and C. F. Van Loan, *Matrix Computations* — the
  standard reference for *computing* the SVD stably and for the truncation tolerances the
  pitfalls here gesture at.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *How do you pick $k$?* — a
  statistical question (scree plots, cross-validation) owned by Unsupervised Learning.
  *What is the smooth alternative to hard truncation?* — Tikhonov/ridge regularization,
  [REGR-14], which replaces $1/\sigma_i$ by $\sigma_i/(\sigma_i^2+\lambda)$. *What happens
  when most matrix entries are missing?* — matrix completion, a non-convex relative of
  Eckart–Young where the clean SVD optimum no longer applies.
